title: practical english usage - Part 90
content:
'experience*.

Get + object + past participle can sometimes be used in the sense of 4 experience:       We    got    our    roof     blown       off.

Have is used in a similar structure, ►     109.2.

I'm going to get my hair cut this afternoon.

I must get my watch repaired. (= 1 want my  watch to be repaired.) somebody else’ . The  past participle has a passive meaning.

Get + object + past participle can mean 'cause something to be done by 3 causative:      get    something           done grammar »110      hear, see, etc + object + verb form Passive forms of watch and notice are not used in this way.

She was seen walking away from the accident.

Justice must not only be done; it must be seen to be done.

He was never heard to say 'thank you' in his life, (     He-was never infinitive has to.

These structures can be used after passive forms of hear and see. In this case, the not I could see Jack getting on the bus. (     l eould see-jack-get-. ..) only the -ing structure is used.

After can see/hear (which refer to actions and events that are in progress, ►    84), I saw her throwing stones at the other children.

A  progressive form can suggest repetition.

I saw the book lying on the table, (     J-saw-the-boek-Ue-. . .) not - I heard the bomb explode, (     Hheard-tbe-bomb-exploding;) I like to watch people walking in the street.

- Watch me jump  over the stream.

As I walked past his room I heard him  talking on the phone.

- I once heard him  give a talk on Japanese politics.

I saw her crossing the road. (= I saw her in the middle, on her way across.) - I saw her cross the road. (= ɪ  saw her cross it from one side to the other.) we  hear or see something in progress, going on. Compare: that we  hear or see the whole of an action or event; an -ing form suggests that There is often a difference of meaning. After these verbs, an infinitive suggests not (     I heard him-went down-thc-stairsz) or I heard him  go down the stairs,  I heard him  going down the stairs.

object + infinitive (without to) or object + -ing form.

Hear, see, watch, notice and similar verbs of perception can be followed by 1 / heard       him     go/going.

110 hear,     see,    etc   + object     + ve  rb  form I won't have my house turned into a hotel.

I won't have you telling me what to do.

I won't have + object + verb form can mean 'I won't allow ...' 5 I won't      have      ...

I had my car stolen last week.

We had our roof blown off in the storm.

Again, the past participle has a passive meaning.

Have + object + past participle can also be used in the sense of 'experience'.

4 experience:       We    had     our    roof     blown       off.

tenses (►   3.2).

happening). This is like the difference between simple and progressive that happened), and the -ing form in the last two (for things that are/were Note the difference between the infinitive in the first two examples (for things I looked up and found we had water dripping through the ceiling.

It's lovely to have children playing in the garden again.

Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles       after    Nouns,    Verbs,   etc    Section 9 grammar »110     hear, see, etc + object + verb form For the difference between hear and listen, >481.    For see, look and watch, ►    575.

For more about verbs that can be foUowed by both infinitives and -ing forms, ►     105.

Look at him  eating! Look at him  eat! (AmE) object + infinitive.

Look at can be followed by object + -ing form, and in American English also by 4 Look    at   him     eating!

I woke up to hear the bedroom door being opened slowly.

As I watched the tree being cut down ...

(being + past participle).

The  idea of 'action or event in progress' can be given by a progressive form Have you ever seen a television thrown through a window?

1 heard my name repeated several times. (= My   name was repeated.) In this structure, the past participle has a passive meaning.

3   I heard       my    name       repeated.

/ saw Chloe talking to Mia. (     I saw Chloe's talking to Mia.) After these verbs, possessives cannot be used with -ing forms.
